name: Gil Evans
description: American composer, arranger, bandleader, and pianist (1912–1988)
abstract: Ian Ernest Gilmore Evans (né Green; May 13, 1912 – March 20, 1988) was a Canadian–American jazz pianist, arranger, composer and bandleader. He is widely recognized as one of the greatest orchestrators in jazz, playing an important role in the development of cool jazz, modal jazz, free jazz, and jazz fusion.

works_created.t: Waltz / Variation on the Misery / So Long

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Malone
about_quotes.evidence: playing with Gil Evans that night I started playing with Gil Evans for the last 15 years of Gil’s life. Toured all over the world with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom Malone interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 3, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Levin
about_quotes.evidence: My first stint with Gil Evans I was playing French horn... I was in the band for fifteen years.
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Levin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Danbury, Connecticut, November 20, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: Gil Evans was one of my influences there. He had a wonderful approach in that regard.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Rudy Van Gelder
about_quotes.evidence: I remember Gil Evans did his first session as a leader in Hackensack... Neither Gil or myself liked the results of that one... So back to Gil Evans, he did the recording that neither of you were happy with. And his next recording was when he came here... Yes, he came here. What was the name of that
about_quotes.doc_title: Rudy Van Gelder — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: I met Hal through the dates with Gil Evans and Teddy Charles. ... Gil was the one who was writing the music on either Teddy Charles’ date or Hal McKusick’s date, and Gil wrote the arrangements. That’s when I first met Gil. I think I made maybe one or two other albums with him of the same type.
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: the most complicated, difficult music which Gil [Evans] had written from Gershwin’s music... Gil Evans didn’t know that, and we didn’t know that. Miles didn’t know that, because it was separated by these separate dates... But he always ran to me wherever he had – it was amazing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
